skills.ref: AP Art History Content Area 1: Global Prehistory (30,000-500 BCE)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Prehistoric Art for AP Art History students (AP Art History Content Area 1: Global Prehistory, 30,000-500 BCE). Students should understand the earliest known artistic expressions and their significance. Key concepts: cave paintings at Lascaux and Altamira demonstrate sophisticated artistic skills in depicting animals and hunting scenes; the Venus of Willendorf and other fertility figures reflect concerns about reproduction and survival; Stonehenge represents monumental architecture aligned with celestial events; the Ambum Stone from Papua New Guinea shows early carving traditions; debate over the purpose of prehistoric art (ritual, communication, magic, decoration, teaching); art as evidence of symbolic thinking and collective identity. Students should analyze prehistoric artworks using the skills of visual analysis (form, content, context, function) without imposing modern interpretations. Required images from the AP 250: Apollo 11 stones, Great Hall of the Bulls (Lascaux), Camelid sacrum in the shape of a canine, Running Horned Woman, Stonehenge. Vocabulary: Paleolithic, Neolithic, megalith, post-and-lintel, Venus figure, cave painting, fertility, ritual, symbolic.

skills.ref: AP Art History Content Area 2: Ancient Mediterranean (3500 BCE-300 CE)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Ancient Art from Egypt, Mesopotamia, Greece, and Rome for AP Art History students (AP Art History Content Area 2: Ancient Mediterranean, 3500 BCE-300 CE). Students should understand the major artistic traditions of the ancient Mediterranean world. Key concepts: Egyptian art's emphasis on permanence, the afterlife, and hierarchical scale (pyramids, temples, wall paintings, sculpture following canonical proportions); Mesopotamian art reflecting power and religion (ziggurats, palace reliefs, cylinder seals, steles like the Code of Hammurabi); Greek art's evolution from Archaic (kouros/kore) to Classical (idealized naturalism, contrapposto, Parthenon) to Hellenistic (dramatic emotion, movement); Roman art combining Greek ideals with verism (realistic portraiture), engineering innovation (arches, concrete, Colosseum, Pantheon), and political propaganda. Students should compare artistic conventions across cultures, analyze how art served political and religious power, and trace the evolution of naturalism. Vocabulary: hierarchical scale, canon of proportions, contrapposto, verism, fresco, relief, column orders, arch, concrete, idealization.

skills.ref: AP Art History Content Area 3: Early Europe and Colonial Americas (200-1750 CE)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Medieval Art for AP Art History students (AP Art History Content Area 3: Early Europe and Colonial Americas, 200-1750 CE). Students should understand the diverse artistic traditions of the medieval period spanning approximately 500-1400 CE. Key concepts: Early Christian art adapted Roman forms for Christian content (catacombs, basilica architecture, mosaics); Byzantine art emphasized spiritual transcendence through icons, gold backgrounds, and Hagia Sophia's innovative dome; Romanesque art features thick walls, rounded arches, sculptural programs on church facades, and manuscript illumination; Gothic art introduced pointed arches, ribbed vaults, flying buttresses, stained glass windows (Chartres Cathedral), and soaring vertical spaces filled with light; Islamic art developed geometric patterns, arabesques, calligraphy, and mosque architecture (no figural representation in religious contexts). Students should analyze how medieval art served religious devotion and institutional power, and understand the relationship between architectural innovation and theological meaning. Vocabulary: basilica, mosaic, icon, Romanesque, Gothic, flying buttress, ribbed vault, stained glass, illuminated manuscript, arabesque.

skills.ref: AP Art History Content Area 3: Early Europe and Colonial Americas (200-1750 CE)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Renaissance Art for AP Art History students (AP Art History Content Area 3: Early Europe and Colonial Americas, 200-1750 CE). Students should understand the artistic revolution of the 15th-16th centuries centered in Italy and spreading across Europe. Key concepts: the revival of classical Greek and Roman ideals (humanism, naturalism, proportion); the development of linear perspective (Brunelleschi, Alberti) creating the illusion of three-dimensional space on a flat surface; key artists and works (Botticelli's Birth of Venus, Leonardo's Last Supper and Mona Lisa, Michelangelo's David and Sistine Chapel ceiling, Raphael's School of Athens); the Northern Renaissance differed in style (oil painting technique, detailed realism, genre scenes) with artists like Jan van Eyck, Albrecht Durer, and Hieronymus Bosch; the role of patronage (Medici family, popes, guilds) in supporting artistic production; the artist's rising social status from craftsman to creative genius. Students should analyze how Renaissance art reflects humanist values and scientific observation. Vocabulary: Renaissance, humanism, linear perspective, vanishing point, sfumato, chiaroscuro, contrapposto, patronage, fresco, oil painting.

skills.ref: AP Art History Content Area 3: Early Europe and Colonial Americas (200-1750 CE)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Baroque Art for AP Art History students (AP Art History Content Area 3: Early Europe and Colonial Americas, 200-1750 CE). Students should understand the dramatic, emotional, and dynamic art of the 17th century. Key concepts: Baroque art emerged partly as a response to the Protestant Reformation, with the Catholic Counter-Reformation commissioning art to inspire faith through sensory experience; characteristics include dramatic lighting (tenebrism/chiaroscuro), dynamic composition, theatrical emotion, rich color, and grandeur; key artists include Caravaggio (dramatic realism, tenebrism), Bernini (dynamic sculpture, theatrical architecture like St. Peter's colonnade), Rubens (dynamic, fleshy figures), Rembrandt (psychological depth, masterful light), and Velazquez (royal portraiture); Baroque architecture features curved facades, elaborate interiors, and illusionistic ceilings; Versailles represents Baroque political power. Students should analyze how Baroque art serves religious, political, and emotional purposes through its sensory intensity. Vocabulary: Baroque, tenebrism, chiaroscuro, Counter-Reformation, dramatic, dynamic composition, illusionism, grandeur, colonnade, theatricality.

skills.ref: AP Art History Content Area 4: Later Europe and Americas (1750-1980 CE)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Rococo Art for AP Art History students (AP Art History Content Area 4: Later Europe and Americas, 1750-1980 CE). Students should understand the light, playful, and ornamental style that developed from the Baroque in early 18th-century France. Key concepts: Rococo emerged as aristocratic taste moved from the grandeur of Versailles to intimate Parisian salons; characteristics include pastel colors, curving asymmetrical forms, playful subjects (fetes galantes, pastoral scenes, mythological love stories), delicate ornamentation, and lighter, more intimate scale; key artists include Watteau (dreamy, poetic scenes), Boucher (sensual mythological subjects), Fragonard (The Swing as the quintessential Rococo work); Rococo decorative arts (furniture, porcelain, tapestries) are equally important; Rococo was criticized by Enlightenment thinkers as frivolous and decadent, leading to the Neoclassical reaction. Students should analyze how Rococo art reflects the values of the French aristocracy before the Revolution and how style relates to social context. Vocabulary: Rococo, fete galante, pastel, asymmetry, ornament, salon, aristocratic, intimate, decorative arts, frivolity.

skills.ref: AP Art History Content Area 4: Later Europe and Americas (1750-1980 CE)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Neoclassical Art for AP Art History students (AP Art History Content Area 4: Later Europe and Americas, 1750-1980 CE). Students should understand the revival of classical Greek and Roman forms driven by Enlightenment ideals in the late 18th century. Key concepts: inspired by archaeological discoveries at Pompeii and Herculaneum and by Enlightenment values of reason, virtue, and civic duty; characteristics include clarity, order, symmetry, restrained emotion, classical subjects (ancient history, mythology), and moral seriousness; key artists include Jacques-Louis David (Oath of the Horatii emphasizing civic virtue, Death of Marat as revolutionary propaganda), Antonio Canova (idealized classical sculpture), and architects (Thomas Jefferson's Monticello and the U.S. Capitol drawing on classical forms). Neoclassicism served political purposes during the French Revolution and American Republic by associating contemporary governments with ancient democratic and republican ideals. Students should analyze how Neoclassicism used the past to comment on the present. Vocabulary: Neoclassicism, classical revival, civic virtue, symmetry, order, reason, Enlightenment, republic, idealization, archaeological.

skills.ref: AP Art History Content Area 4: Later Europe and Americas (1750-1980 CE)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Romantic Art for AP Art History students (AP Art History Content Area 4: Later Europe and Americas, 1750-1980 CE). Students should understand the artistic movement that emphasized emotion, individualism, and the power of nature in reaction to Neoclassical rationalism. Key concepts: Romanticism valued imagination over reason, emotion over intellect, and the individual over society; the sublime (awe-inspiring, terrifying aspects of nature that overwhelm human comprehension) was a central concept; key artists include Delacroix (Liberty Leading the People, dramatic color and movement), Gericault (Raft of the Medusa, human suffering), Friedrich (solitary figures in vast landscapes), Turner (atmospheric light and color dissolving form), and Goya (dark psychological explorations, political critique); Romantic landscape painting elevated nature to a spiritual subject; the movement also embraced exotic settings, medieval themes, and political revolution. Students should analyze how Romanticism challenged Enlightenment rationalism and expressed modern anxieties. Vocabulary: Romanticism, sublime, imagination, emotion, individualism, landscape, nature, exotic, medieval revival, Sturm und Drang.

skills.ref: AP Art History Content Area 4: Later Europe and Americas (1750-1980 CE)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Realism and Impressionism for AP Art History students (AP Art History Content Area 4: Later Europe and Americas, 1750-1980 CE). Students should understand how these mid-to-late 19th century movements revolutionized art by depicting contemporary life. Key concepts: Realism (1840s-1880s) depicted everyday subjects without idealization; Courbet rejected academic conventions by painting workers and peasants at monumental scale; Daumier used art for social criticism; Realism was influenced by photography, positivism, and social consciousness. Impressionism (1860s-1880s) captured fleeting moments of light and atmosphere using visible brushstrokes, bright colors, and outdoor painting (en plein air); key artists include Monet (serial paintings of haystacks, cathedrals, water lilies capturing changing light), Renoir (leisure scenes), Degas (ballet dancers, unusual compositions influenced by photography and Japanese prints), and Cassatt (women and children). Students should analyze how both movements broke from academic tradition and responded to modernity. Vocabulary: Realism, Impressionism, en plein air, visible brushstroke, light, color theory, broken color, composition, modernity, academic art.

skills.ref: AP Art History Content Area 4: Later Europe and Americas (1750-1980 CE)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Post-Impressionism for AP Art History students (AP Art History Content Area 4: Later Europe and Americas, 1750-1980 CE). Students should understand the diverse artistic responses that built upon and moved beyond Impressionism in the late 19th century. Key concepts: Post-Impressionism is not a unified style but rather individual artistic visions that rejected Impressionism's emphasis on surface appearances while retaining its color innovations; Cezanne sought to reveal underlying geometric structure in nature (bridge to Cubism); Van Gogh used expressive color and energetic brushwork to convey emotion (bridge to Expressionism); Gauguin pursued spiritual and symbolic meaning through simplified forms and non-Western influences (bridge to Symbolism); Seurat developed Pointillism (systematic application of dots of pure color); Toulouse-Lautrec depicted Parisian nightlife with bold graphic style. Students should analyze how each Post-Impressionist artist transformed Impressionist innovations in a personal direction, laying the groundwork for 20th-century modernism. Vocabulary: Post-Impressionism, structure, expression, symbolism, Pointillism, subjective color, geometric form, primitivism, synthetism, modernism.

skills.ref: AP Art History Content Area 4: Later Europe and Americas (1750-1980 CE)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Early Modern Art for AP Art History students (AP Art History Content Area 4: Later Europe and Americas, 1750-1980 CE). Students should understand the radical artistic experimentation of the early 20th century that broke from representational traditions. Key concepts: Fauvism (Matisse) used wild, non-naturalistic color for emotional expression; Expressionism (Kirchner, Munch, Kandinsky) distorted forms to express inner psychological states; Cubism (Picasso, Braque) shattered single-viewpoint perspective to show multiple perspectives simultaneously (Analytic and Synthetic phases); Futurism celebrated speed, technology, and modern life; Constructivism and Suprematism (Malevich, Tatlin) pursued pure geometric abstraction; Dada (Duchamp, readymades like Fountain) challenged the very definition of art and questioned artistic conventions. Students should understand how these movements responded to modernity, industrialization, and the trauma of World War I, and how each redefined what art could be. Vocabulary: Fauvism, Expressionism, Cubism, Futurism, Dada, abstraction, readymade, avant-garde, non-representational, modernism.

skills.ref: AP Art History Content Area 4: Later Europe and Americas (1750-1980 CE)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Modern Art Movements including Cubism, Surrealism, and others for AP Art History students (AP Art History Content Area 4: Later Europe and Americas, 1750-1980 CE). Students should understand the major art movements of the 20th century and their philosophical foundations. Key concepts: Surrealism (influenced by Freudian psychoanalysis) explored the unconscious mind through dreamlike imagery (Dali, Magritte) and automatic techniques (Miro, Ernst); Abstract Expressionism (post-WWII New York) emphasized the act of painting itself through gestural abstraction (Pollock's drip paintings, de Kooning) and color field painting (Rothko, Newman); Pop Art (Warhol, Lichtenstein) embraced mass culture, consumer products, and mechanical reproduction; Minimalism (Judd, Flavin) reduced art to essential geometric forms; Conceptual Art (Kosuth, Weiner) prioritized the idea over the physical object. Students should analyze how each movement redefined art's purpose, materials, and relationship to society. Vocabulary: Surrealism, Abstract Expressionism, Pop Art, Minimalism, Conceptual Art, automatism, gestural, color field, mass culture, readymade.

skills.ref: AP Art History Content Area 10: Global Contemporary (1980-Present)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Postmodern Art for AP Art History students (AP Art History Content Area 10: Global Contemporary, 1980-Present). Students should understand the shift from modernist certainties to postmodern plurality and critique. Key concepts: postmodernism rejects the idea of a single artistic truth or progress narrative; characteristics include appropriation (borrowing from art history and popular culture), pastiche (mixing styles), irony, deconstruction of established hierarchies (high art vs. low culture, Western vs. non-Western), and questioning of authorship and originality; key artists include Cindy Sherman (identity and representation through self-portraiture), Barbara Kruger (text-based critique of consumerism and power), Jean-Michel Basquiat (graffiti-influenced critique of race and power structures), Jeff Koons (appropriation of kitsch); postmodernism embraces diversity of voices, media, and perspectives. Students should analyze how postmodern art challenges viewers' assumptions about art, beauty, truth, and cultural authority. Vocabulary: postmodernism, appropriation, pastiche, deconstruction, irony, identity, representation, pluralism, kitsch, critique, installation.

skills.ref: AP Art History Content Area 10: Global Contemporary (1980-Present)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Contemporary Art for AP Art History students (AP Art History Content Area 10: Global Contemporary, 1980-Present). Students should understand the diverse, global, and technology-driven art practices of the late 20th and early 21st centuries. Key concepts: contemporary art encompasses an enormous range of media (installation, performance, video, digital, bio-art, social practice); globalization has expanded the art world beyond Western centers (Venice Biennale, Documenta, global biennials); identity politics (race, gender, sexuality, nationality) are central themes; site-specific and environmental art (Christo, Maya Lin's Vietnam Veterans Memorial); digital and new media art; relational aesthetics and participatory art; the role of art institutions (museums, galleries, art fairs, auction houses) in defining what counts as art; the commercialization of contemporary art. Students should analyze how contemporary artists respond to globalization, technology, and social issues, and evaluate art using multiple interpretive frameworks. Vocabulary: contemporary, installation, performance art, video art, site-specific, globalization, identity, biennale, relational aesthetics, new media.

skills.ref: AP Art History Content Areas 5-9: Cross-cultural
skills.prompt: This topic covers Global Art Traditions for AP Art History students (AP Art History Content Areas 5-9: Cross-cultural). Students should understand art traditions from Africa, Asia, the Americas, and the Pacific that are often underrepresented in Western-centric art history. Key concepts: African art encompasses diverse traditions including Benin bronzes, Nok terracottas, Kongo power figures, Great Zimbabwe, and textile traditions; South and Southeast Asian art includes Hindu and Buddhist temple architecture (Angkor Wat), sculpture, and painting; East Asian art includes Chinese landscape painting, Japanese woodblock prints (ukiyo-e), calligraphy, and garden design; Indigenous American art includes Mesoamerican (Aztec, Maya), Andean (Inca), and North American traditions; Pacific Island art includes Polynesian navigation, Maori carving, and Aboriginal Australian art. Students should analyze these traditions on their own terms without imposing Western aesthetic criteria and understand how colonialism has affected their preservation and interpretation. Vocabulary: cross-cultural, indigenous, colonialism, appropriation, repatriation, ritual, sacred, functional art, oral tradition, cultural context.

skills.ref: AP Art History Cross-cutting: Architecture across all Content Areas
skills.prompt: This topic covers Architectural History for AP Art History students (AP Art History Cross-cutting: Architecture across all Content Areas). Students should understand the evolution of architectural form, technology, and meaning from ancient times to the present. Key concepts: post-and-lintel construction (ancient temples); the arch, vault, and dome (Roman innovation, later Islamic and Byzantine development); Gothic structural innovation (pointed arch, ribbed vault, flying buttress); Renaissance ideal proportions and centralized plans; Baroque theatrical spaces; the impact of new materials (iron, steel, reinforced concrete, glass) enabling skyscraper construction and modernist design; key modern architects include Wright (Fallingwater, organic architecture), Le Corbusier (pilotis, open plan), Mies van der Rohe (less is more, glass boxes); postmodern architecture reintroduced ornament and historical reference; contemporary architecture (Gehry, Hadid, sustainable design). Students should analyze how architectural choices reflect cultural values, available technology, and environmental considerations. Vocabulary: post-and-lintel, arch, vault, dome, buttress, load-bearing, steel frame, curtain wall, organic architecture, sustainable design.

skills.ref: AP Art History Cross-cutting: Sculpture across all Content Areas
skills.prompt: This topic covers Sculptural Traditions for AP Art History students (AP Art History Cross-cutting: Sculpture across all Content Areas). Students should understand the evolution of sculpture as a three-dimensional art form across cultures and time periods. Key concepts: ancient sculpture from freestanding Egyptian statues (frontal, rigid) to Greek naturalism (contrapposto, idealized forms) to Roman verism (realistic portraiture); materials and techniques (stone carving, bronze casting using lost-wax method, clay modeling, wood carving, assemblage); relief sculpture (bas-relief and high relief) on architectural surfaces; Renaissance sculpture (Donatello's David, Michelangelo's David and Pieta) revived classical ideals; Bernini's Baroque dynamism; Rodin's modern expressiveness; 20th-century innovations include Brancusi's abstraction, Calder's mobiles (kinetic sculpture), Giacometti's existential figures, and minimalist sculpture (Judd, Serra). Students should analyze sculpture through form, material, technique, scale, and spatial relationship to the viewer and environment. Vocabulary: freestanding, relief, contrapposto, lost-wax casting, carving, modeling, kinetic, installation, public sculpture, site-specific, pedestal.

skills.ref: AP Art History Cross-cutting: Patronage and Art Making
skills.prompt: This topic covers the Role of Patronage for AP Art History students (AP Art History Cross-cutting: Patronage and Art Making). Students should understand how the economic and social systems supporting art production have shaped what art gets made and who makes it. Key concepts: patronage systems throughout history include royal and aristocratic patronage (Egyptian pharaohs, Medici family, Louis XIV), religious patronage (Catholic Church commissions, Buddhist monasteries, Islamic courts), civic patronage (Greek temples, Roman public works), and modern patronage (museums, collectors, galleries, government grants, corporate sponsorship); the patron's influence on subject matter, style, and scale; the tension between artistic freedom and patron demands; the shift from patronage to the art market (galleries, auction houses, art fairs); the artist's social status evolving from anonymous craftsman to celebrity genius; art as investment and commodity in the contemporary world. Students should analyze how patronage systems reflect power structures and shape artistic production. Vocabulary: patronage, commission, patron, guild, academy, art market, gallery, collector, auction, artistic freedom, commodity.

skills.ref: AP Art History Cross-cutting: Art and Social Commentary
skills.prompt: This topic covers Art and Social Change for AP Art History students (AP Art History Cross-cutting: Art and Social Commentary). Students should understand how artists have used their work to critique, challenge, and transform social and political conditions. Key concepts: art as propaganda (Roman imperial portraits, revolutionary art, Soviet Socialist Realism, Nazi-approved art); art as protest (Goya's Third of May 1808, Picasso's Guernica, Diego Rivera's murals addressing class struggle, Ai Weiwei's political activism); art and social justice movements (Harlem Renaissance celebrating Black identity, feminist art by Judy Chicago and the Guerrilla Girls, AIDS activism in art, immigration and refugee art); art censorship and controversy (Mapplethorpe, Ofili, Banksy); public art and monuments as contested spaces; street art and graffiti as democratic art forms. Students should analyze how art can both reinforce and challenge power structures and consider the ethics of representing suffering. Vocabulary: propaganda, protest art, social commentary, censorship, activism, mural, public art, political art, documentary, representation.

skills.ref: AP Art History Cross-cutting: Art and Identity
skills.prompt: This topic covers Art and Identity for AP Art History students (AP Art History Cross-cutting: Art and Identity). Students should understand how art expresses, constructs, and questions personal and collective identity. Key concepts: self-portraiture as a vehicle for exploring identity (Rembrandt, Frida Kahlo, Cindy Sherman); art expressing racial and ethnic identity (African American art from the Harlem Renaissance to contemporary artists like Kehinde Wiley and Kara Walker); gender and sexuality in art (feminist art challenging the male gaze, LGBTQ artists using art to claim visibility); national and cultural identity expressed through art (Mexican muralism, Indigenous art as cultural preservation); art and diaspora (how migration and displacement shape artistic expression); the politics of representation (who is depicted, by whom, and how); art as a means of cultural preservation and resistance against assimilation. Students should analyze how identity categories (race, gender, class, nationality, sexuality) intersect in artistic production and reception. Vocabulary: identity, self-portrait, representation, male gaze, feminism, diaspora, cultural identity, intersectionality, visibility, agency.
